content.text: Both interface and type alias define named types in TypeScript. They share significant overlap — both can describe object shapes, support generics, and be used as function parameter types. The differences matter in specific scenarios, and knowing them helps you choose the right tool for each situation.

attrs.alt: interface vs type alias capability comparison diagram

content.text: TypeScript merges multiple interface declarations with the same name in the same scope. This makes interfaces open for augmentation — useful when extending third-party types or the global Window object.
Primitive, Tuple, and Template Literal Aliases
that classes will implement (component inputs, service contracts, route guards)
such as extending Express.Request or Window
— interfaces give consumers cleaner hover types and better error messages
— merging lets you add members without touching the original declaration
from another via mapped or conditional logic —
The interface vs type choice is mostly stylistic for object shapes. Let the feature decide: if you need a union, tuple, mapped type, or conditional type, reach for
. If you need declaration merging or class implementation contracts, reach for
. In an Angular codebase, service contracts and DI tokens favour interfaces; component state and result shapes favour type aliases.
How TypeScript Displays Each in Errors

One practical difference between interfaces and type aliases is how TypeScript renders them in error messages and hover tooltips. Interfaces display their name; type aliases may display the full expanded type.

The structures are interchangeable for object shapes, but the refactor is one-directional in some cases: converting an interface to a type alias is always possible, but converting a type alias to an interface fails if the alias uses unions, tuples, or mapped types.

At scale, interfaces have a compiler advantage over intersections of type aliases. TypeScript can cache and name-check an interface, while a type alias intersection forces the compiler to structurally expand and compare the full set of properties on each use.
The TypeScript team's own performance tip:
prefer interface extends over type intersections
when composing large object hierarchies. The compiler memoises interface types by name; type intersections are re-computed eagerly.
both describe object shapes and support generics — structurally interchangeable for most object types.
can represent unions, intersections of arbitrary types, tuples, primitives, mapped types, and conditional types.
supports declaration merging and is the idiomatic choice for class contracts and third-party augmentation.
Angular service contracts, DI tokens, and class implementations →
Component state unions, HTTP result shapes, utility types →
Error messages from interfaces tend to be cleaner because TypeScript displays the interface name; type aliases sometimes display the expanded definition.
